content.text: is an HTTP server that sits between your application and one or more LLM providers, exposing an OpenAI-compatible
— no provider keys ever reach application code.

attrs.alt: Model proxy architecture — apps talk to a local proxy which handles auth, logging, caching, and routing to multiple providers

content.text: Any existing OpenAI SDK client (Python, Node.js, curl) can point at the proxy by changing
to the proxy address. No other code changes are required.
provider API keys live in one place (the proxy); developers get short-lived virtual keys.
enforce per-user or per-team token budgets at the network layer.
the proxy retries on a backup model when the primary provider is rate-limited or down.
return cached responses for semantically similar prompts, cutting cost and latency.
every prompt and response is logged centrally for compliance and debugging.
route a percentage of traffic to a new model without application-side changes.
The LiteLLM Proxy is the most popular self-hosted option. It reads a YAML config, starts a FastAPI server, and exposes an OpenAI-compatible API.

# litellm_config.yaml
model_list:
  - model_name: gpt-4o-mini
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/gpt-4o-mini
      api_key: os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY

  - model_name: claude-sonnet
    litellm_params:
      model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-5
      api_key: os.environ/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

  - model_name: llama-local
    litellm_params:
      model: ollama/llama3.2
      api_base: http://localhost:11434

general_settings:
  master_key: sk-proxy-key
  max_budget: 100.0         # USD cap across all keys
  budget_duration: 30d

# Start the proxy (listens on :4000 by default)
litellm --config litellm_config.yaml --port 4000

# In a separate terminal — test with curl
curl http://localhost:4000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer sk-proxy-key' \
  -d '{
    "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}]
  }'
) to the real provider model ID (
). All response shapes conform to the OpenAI schema.
Connecting an Application to the Proxy

from openai import OpenAI

# Point the standard OpenAI client at the local proxy
client = OpenAI(
    base_url='http://localhost:4000',
    api_key='sk-proxy-key',   # the master key from litellm_config.yaml
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='claude-sonnet',    # alias from the config, not the real model ID
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain gradient descent.'}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
The proxy URL and master key are the only things that change. All model-specific code (streaming, tool calling, structured output) works unchanged.
In production, put the proxy behind TLS (nginx / Caddy) and store the master key in a secrets manager. Never expose the proxy's port to the public internet without authentication.
The proxy's admin API lets you issue
with per-key spend limits, model allow-lists, and expiry. Developers use their virtual key; the proxy holds the real provider keys.

# Create a virtual key with a spend limit via the proxy's admin API
import httpx

key = httpx.post(
    'http://localhost:4000/key/generate',
    headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer sk-proxy-key'},
    json={
        'models': ['gpt-4o-mini', 'claude-sonnet'],   # allowed models
        'max_budget': 5.0,         # USD spend limit for this key
        'budget_duration': '7d',
        'metadata': {'user': 'alice@example.com'},
    },
).json()

print('Virtual key:', key['key'])   # sk-abc123...
When a virtual key hits its budget cap, the proxy returns a
error. The calling application handles this like any rate limit — no special proxy integration needed.
Helicone is a SaaS proxy that adds prompt logging, cost tracking, and analytics. The integration is a single
change; your real API key travels to OpenAI, while Helicone intercepts request/response pairs.

# Using Helicone as a transparent proxy — only base_url changes
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url='https://oai.helicone.ai/v1',
    api_key='sk-openai-...',         # your real OpenAI key
    default_headers={
        'Helicone-Auth': 'Bearer sk-helicone-...',
        'Helicone-Property-Environment': 'production',
        'Helicone-Property-Feature': 'chatbot',
    },
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Summarise this document...'}],
)
) appear as filterable dimensions in the Helicone dashboard — useful for attributing cost to features, environments, or users.
nginx as a Simple Reverse Proxy
For minimal deployments where you only need
(replace a dummy client-side key with the real provider key), a few lines of nginx config suffice.
This pattern keeps provider keys on a server you control without the overhead of a full proxy server. It does not add routing, caching, or rate limiting — for those, use LiteLLM Proxy.
LiteLLM Proxy supports Redis-backed semantic caching: instead of matching prompts by exact string, it embeds the prompt and returns cached responses for queries above a cosine similarity threshold.

# litellm_config.yaml — semantic caching
litellm_settings:
  cache: true
  cache_params:
    type: redis              # requires a running Redis
    host: localhost
    port: 6379
    similarity_threshold: 0.95   # cosine similarity for semantic cache hit

# The proxy answers from cache if a semantically similar prompt was seen recently
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:4000', api_key='sk-proxy-key')

# First call — hits the LLM
r1 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'What is the capital of France?'}],
)
print(r1.choices[0].message.content)    # Paris

# Second call — phrased differently but semantically equivalent → cache hit
r2 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Name the capital city of France.'}],
)
print(r2.choices[0].message.content)    # Paris (from cache, faster + free)
Semantic caching is most effective for FAQ-style queries where users rephrase the same question. Avoid it for prompts that must return real-time data or where correctness depends on exact wording.
Teams needing full control, budget enforcement, local models
Quick logging and analytics without infra
No-infra multi-provider routing, single billing account
nginx buffers responses by default, which breaks streaming. Add
the proxy makes outbound HTTPS calls to providers. Ensure the proxy host can reach
, etc., and that CA certs are current.
semantic cache hits return stale responses for time-sensitive queries. Add a
header from the client for real-time routes.
virtual keys are still credentials. Rotate them on employee offboarding and set short
LiteLLM Proxy does not hot-reload its YAML. Changes to
require a restart. Use a process manager (systemd, Docker Compose) for zero-downtime restarts.
block controls how traffic is distributed and when the proxy falls back to a secondary model. The
threshold marks a model as temporarily unhealthy after repeated errors.

# litellm_config.yaml — fallback routing configuration
model_list:
  - model_name: primary-chat
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/gpt-4o
      api_key: os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY
      timeout: 20

  - model_name: fallback-chat
    litellm_params:
      model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-5
      api_key: os.environ/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

router_settings:
  fallbacks:
    - primary-chat:
        - fallback-chat
  num_retries: 3
  retry_after: 5          # seconds between retries
  routing_strategy: least-busy
  allowed_fails: 2        # errors before marking a model unhealthy
routing strategy distributes requests to whichever model alias has the fewest outstanding calls, preventing thundering-herd failures during traffic spikes.
response header with the actual model that served the request. Log this in your application to track which fallback triggered and at what rate.
Guardrails — Content Moderation and PII Redaction

The proxy's guardrail layer runs before and/or after each call to scan for prompt injection, policy violations, or sensitive data. Guardrails are configured per proxy instance and invisible to application code.

# litellm_config.yaml — guardrails (content moderation, PII redaction)
litellm_settings:
  guardrails:
    - guardrail_name: 'lakera-guard'
      litellm_params:
        guardrail: lakera_guard
        mode: 'during_call'   # pre, post, or during_call
        api_key: os.environ/LAKERA_API_KEY

    - guardrail_name: 'pii-masker'
      litellm_params:
        guardrail: hide_secrets
        mode: 'pre_call'      # redact before the prompt leaves the proxy
Available guardrail integrations include Lakera Guard (prompt injection), AWS Bedrock Guardrails, and the built-in
filter that regex-redacts common secret patterns (API keys, AWS credentials, connection strings) from prompts and responses.
Before the request reaches the LLM
Building a Custom Proxy with FastAPI

When you need logic that LiteLLM does not support — custom authentication schemes, bespoke PII handling, or proprietary routing rules — a few dozen lines of FastAPI give you a fully compliant OpenAI-compatible proxy.

# A minimal custom proxy built with FastAPI
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import httpx
import os

app = FastAPI()

UPSTREAM = 'https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions'
OPENAI_KEY = os.environ['OPENAI_API_KEY']


@app.post('/v1/chat/completions')
async def proxy(request: Request):
    # 1. Authenticate the incoming key
    auth = request.headers.get('Authorization', '')
    if auth != 'Bearer my-internal-key':
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail='Unauthorized')

    body = await request.json()

    # 2. Optional: redact PII, log the prompt, apply guardrails here
    print('User prompt:', body['messages'][-1]['content'][:120])

    # 3. Forward to upstream with the real key
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        upstream_resp = await client.post(
            UPSTREAM,
            json=body,
            headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {OPENAI_KEY}'},
            timeout=60,
        )

    return upstream_resp.json()
This pattern is the foundation of all commercial proxies. Extend it with a Redis cache, a database for prompt logging, or an async task queue for post-call analysis.
Streaming Support in a Custom Proxy
Streaming requires forwarding the server-sent event (SSE) byte stream as-is.
gives async access to the raw bytes;
passes them to the client unchanged.

# Streaming proxy — forward the server-sent event stream from upstream
@app.post('/v1/chat/completions/stream')
async def proxy_stream(request: Request):
    body = await request.json()
    body['stream'] = True

    async def generate():
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            async with client.stream(
                'POST', UPSTREAM,
                json=body,
                headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {OPENAI_KEY}'},
                timeout=None,
            ) as resp:
                async for chunk in resp.aiter_bytes():
                    yield chunk

    return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type='text/event-stream')
Do not buffer chunks before yielding them — buffering defeats the purpose of streaming and can cause client-side timeout errors on long responses.
sends a percentage of traffic to each model. Log the
header to correlate model assignment with quality metrics.

# litellm_config.yaml — traffic splitting for A/B testing
router_settings:
  model_group_alias:
    experiment-chat:
      - model_name: gpt-4o-mini
        weight: 70        # 70% of traffic
      - model_name: claude-haiku
        weight: 30        # 30% of traffic
Pair traffic splitting with an evaluation pipeline — record user feedback or LLM-as-judge scores per model, then shift weight toward the better-performing variant.
A model proxy is an OpenAI-compatible HTTP server that centralises credentials, rate limiting, routing, caching, and logging.
LiteLLM Proxy is the most capable self-hosted option: multi-provider routing, virtual keys, semantic caching, and budget enforcement from a YAML config.
Helicone provides SaaS-level observability (prompt logs, cost analytics, A/B testing) with a one-line
nginx serves as a lightweight key-injection proxy when full routing logic is not needed.
for semantic caching to avoid serving stale real-time responses.
Virtual keys from LiteLLM Proxy keep provider API keys off developer machines and enforce per-user spend limits.
